entry.title: Re: How to optimise query performance in sap datasphere i...
entry.content:
Hi,

Without persistency the performance optimization may be a challenge. First check if a simple view directly from SNOW is fast enough, this way you ensure the connection is OK and working for virtualization. 

If the basic check is fast enough, then go for the Views self. Best approach is the HANA PlanViz. You can get it via DB Access and then analyze in Eclipse / HANA Studio. PlanViz is like Exec Plan, just far more powerful. You can find a lot of blogs how to analyze PlanViz (just enable the critical path and complexity of PlanViz will be reduced). 

Approach without persistency is basically as in any DB performance analyzes. 

Hope this helps. Again without persistency, it may be a challenge.

Cheers, Martin

entry.title: Re: Direct Email out of Customer Invoices in Financial Ac...
entry.content:
Short answer: SAP21 is an on‑demand correspondence — it is not e‑mailed automatically when the FI invoice is posted, and API‑posted invoices are not even eligible for it until you add the BAdI from note 3757894 as you mentioned. So the "automatic e‑mail on creation" you describe is not achievable with standard SAP21; the note enables eligibility, not automation.

"Please advise how to set up the automatic e‑mail output of SAP21 for invoices created via API"

No supported — not as automatic output. Standard SAP21 is issued on demand from the Create Correspondence app (or Manage Customer Line Items → Create Correspondence). There is no event that emits SAP21 automatically when the accounting document is posted, and no mass/scheduled job for it. Reference: Printing of Customer Invoices in FI; and still open influence request 331512. Please vote there to speed up its delivery.

Note: You can configure the e‑mail channel and a fixed dedicated recipient in Output Parameter Determination, but the actual issuing of the PDF stays a manual/on‑demand step. Reference: Configuring the Output of FI Customer Invoices.

"Probably we have to implement note 3757894"

Yes — but only to make your API documents eligible, not to automate sending. Standard SAP21 only accepts documents posted with BKPF‑TCODE = FB70 / FBDC_P020 / FBDC_P021 (i.e. Create Outgoing Invoices or Upload Customer Open Items in public cloud); API/interface‑posted documents fail this origin check and would raise error F0744 if you tried to preview them. Note 3757894 provides BAdI FICUS_INV_ADJUST_FILTER (Custom Logic app, F6957), where you set PRINT_ELIGIBLE = X to allow SAP21 for your API‑posted invoices. Reference: KBA 3757894; KBA 3557212.

Important (BAdI behavior): with your own implementation, the "has a customer line item" and "sales‑related (BSEG‑XUMSW = X)" checks still run — only the TCODE check is dropped (reimplement it if you want to keep it). So your API postings must carry a sales‑related customer line.

"Our understanding is that the e‑mail output is generated automatically"

No — for neither manually created nor API‑created documents. SAP21 is always a correspondence you issue on demand; it is not generated automatically at posting time. The BAdI removes the origin restriction for API documents but adds no automation. 

Final notes:

  • Confirm your API sets a sales‑related customer line (BSEG‑XUMSW = X); otherwise BAdI check (2) still blocks generation.
  • Maintain the dedicated address via the Email Recipient determination step (fixed), not from BP master data.
  • There is no standard trigger/API to auto‑issue SAP21 at posting; if fully automatic dispatch is mandatory, evaluate SD billing output (which does e‑mail automatically on billing‑document creation) or vote and regularly check back influence request 331512.
  • In Public Cloud, "implementing" note 3757894 means building this BAdI via Custom Logic (F6957) — there is no SNOTE.

Summary

Configure the SAP21 e‑mail output (dedicated recipient) and implement BAdI FICUS_INV_ADJUST_FILTER so your API‑posted invoices become eligible — but plan for a manual/on‑demand issuing step in Create Correspondence, because standard SAP21 does not auto‑e‑mail at posting and cannot be mass‑scheduled today.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Building a Conversational AI Agent in S/4HANA — Without Leaving the ABAP Stack
entry.content:
An experiment with two things at once: the yaai open-source ABAP addon and SAP AI Core as the LLM provider. The goal was to see whether a non-coder could wire an LLM directly into SAP S/4HANA — no external APIs, no middleware, and no writing a single line of code.

How This Started

I had two things I wanted to experiment with:

  1. yaai — an open-source ABAP addon by Christian Jianelli that lets you call LLMs from ABAP and register ABAP methods as tools a model can invoke. I wanted to understand what this actually meant in practice, not in theory.
  2. SAP AI Core — SAP's managed LLM platform on BTP. I had access to a running deployment and wanted to see how it behaved as the backend for an ABAP-native agent.

For the business scenario, I needed something simple — not because the scenario mattered, but because I wanted the complexity to come from the integration, not the domain. Batch management in SAP S/4HANA was the first thing that came to mind: logistics personnel ask the same questions every day (What's the shelf life expiration date on this batch? Which batches are about to expire?), the answers are all in standard SAP S/4HANA, and the data model is well understood.

All the ABAP code was written using Claude for Coding inside Visual Studio Code. I described what I needed, reviewed what came back, tested it in the SAP system, and fed errors back in. I did not write a single line of code. All this in less than a day!! My contribution was knowing what I wanted, understanding SAP well enough to validate the results, and a great deal of cutting and pasting.

All source code is available at github.com/davidlincourt-eng/yaai-batch-agent

What yaai Actually Is

yaai is a set of ABAP classes that lets you:

  • Call LLM APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, Ollama, and SAP AI Core) from ABAP
  • Register any ABAP instance method as a "tool" the model can invoke during a conversation
  • Maintain multi-turn conversation history
  • Use system instructions to give the model a persona and scope
  • Run agents with tool calling through a UI

It comes with a companion application, yaai_cockpit, which is a SAPUI5 app you deploy to your SAP S/4HANA system. It gives you a browser-based UI for configuring agents, registering tools, uploading prompt documents, and chatting with the agent — all without touching code.

Architecture in One Diagram

User (browser)
     │
     ▼
yaai_cockpit (SAPUI5, served from SAP S/4HANA)
     │  /sap/yaai/agent  (ICF REST)
     ▼
YCL_AAI_AGENT  ──► ZCL_YAAI_AICORE_CONN  ──► SAP AI Core (gpt-4.1)
     │                  (OAuth2 token)              │
     │                                    tool call │
     ▼                                              ▼
ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS                      ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS_PROXY
(reads MCH1, MARA, INOB, AUSP)            (STRING → typed ABAP params)

Everything runs inside the SAP S/4HANA work process. The LLM decides what to ask; ABAP fetches the answer from the database; the cockpit shows the conversation.

The Key Advantage: No Published APIs

In a conventional integration architecture, making SAP S/4HANA batch data available to an AI application means leveraging the S/4HANA primary standard API for the Batch Master Record (OData service API_BATCH_SRV). It allows external systems to synchronously read, create, and change batch data an OData or REST API.

With the yaai approach, the LLM calls ABAP methods directly, inside the same SAP S/4HANA system. In this case, there is a standard API... but it's not aways the case. That means there is no API to publish, no external endpoint to secure, and no data ever leaves the system boundary to reach the model uncontrolled. The LLM receives only what the tool method returns — a formatted string — not raw table data.

This removes an entire layer of integration and keeps the data access logic in the one place where it has always been: ABAP.

An Important Caveat: Security

This experiment does not implement authorisation checks. The ABAP tool methods (ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS) read directly from the SAP S/4HANA database without verifying that the calling user has the appropriate roles or authorisations.

In a production scenario this is a significant gap. The tool classes should enforce the same authorisation checks that the equivalent SAP S/4HANA transactions enforce — using AUTHORITY-CHECK statements against the relevant authorisation objects (e.g. M_MSEG_WMB for batch display). Without this, any user who can access the cockpit chat can query any batch in any plant, regardless of their SAP role.

This is a proof of concept. Before deploying to a production or regulated SAP S/4HANA environment, authorisation checks must be added to every tool method.

What the Agent Can Answer

Once deployed, a user can ask questions in plain language:

User question Tool called What happens

"What is the SLED for batch 0000000045 of material TG22 in plant 1710?"GET_BATCH_HEADERReads MCH1 for VFDAT
"What classification characteristics does that batch have?"GET_BATCH_CLASSIFICATIONNavigates INOB → AUSP → CABN
"Which batches of TG22 expire in the next 30 days?"FIND_EXPIRING_BATCHESQueries MCH1 with date window
"List all expired batches for TG22 in plant 1710"FIND_EXPIRED_BATCHESFilters MCH1 where VFDAT < today
"Can you book a meeting for me?"—Refused (system instructions scope it)

Building It: The Five Moving Parts

1. The Connection Layer — ZCL_YAAI_AICORE_CONN

SAP AI Core uses OAuth2 client credentials. Rather than storing a static API key, ZCL_YAAI_AICORE_CONN fetches a fresh Bearer token from XSUAA on every call and injects the required AI-Resource-Group header. The token is valid for ~12 hours; since it's fetched per-call, there is no manual key rotation.

Credentials are stored in TVARVC (transaction STVARV) — never hardcoded. See Step 0 of the Setup Guide for the exact entries required.

2. The Tool Classes — ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS and its Proxy

The business logic reads from SAP S/4HANA tables:

" Read batch header — SLED lives in MCH1, not MCHA
SELECT SINGLE matnr, charg, vfdat, hsdat, lwedt
  FROM mch1
  INTO @DATA(ls_mch1)
  WHERE matnr = @i_matnr
    AND charg = @i_charg.
Note: VFDAT (shelf life expiration date) is stored in MCH1 (cross-plant batch table), not in MCHA (plant-level batch table). This is a common source of confusion — MCHA.VFDAT is always blank. Claude for Coding got this wrong on the first attempt and had to be corrected.

Because the LLM passes parameters as strings, a proxy class (ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS_PROXY) handles type conversion before delegating to the real class. This is a yaai pattern that prevents runtime conversion dumps when the model sends "30" where ABAP expects TYPE i.

The full source code for both classes is in the repository: ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS and ZCL_YAAI_BATCH_TOOLS_PROXY.

3. The Agent Persona — System Instructions and Tool Guide

Two markdown files uploaded to the cockpit define how the agent behaves:

  • System instructions — sets the persona ("SAP S/4HANA Batch Management Assistant"), defines scope, and enforces formatting rules (dates as DD.MM.YYYY, "not maintained" for blank characteristics)
  • Tool usage guide — tells the LLM exactly which tool to call for which type of question, with a decision tree and parameter examples

This is the prompt engineering layer. The better these documents are, the more reliably the agent picks the right tool for a given question.

The two documents are in the repository: system instructions and tool usage guide.

4. The Cockpit — Configuring the Agent

The yaai_cockpit provides a browser UI for wiring everything together: registering the LLM API endpoint, defining tools, uploading documents, and creating the agent — all without touching code.

The complete step-by-step cockpit configuration — LLM API setup, tool registration, document upload, and agent creation — is covered in Steps 3 through 7 of the Setup Guide.

5. The Conversation

With everything wired up, the agent is accessible via the cockpit's built-in chat panel or through any custom UI that calls the /sap/yaai/agent ICF endpoint on the SAP S/4HANA system. To see the conversation in action, the project repository includes the full setup guide of the agent responding to batch management questions.

Here is an example of a conversation in the Cockpit

 

What Had to Be Fixed Along the Way

Getting yaai to work with SAP AI Core required a few patches to the yaai framework classes. These were not in the original yaai documentation — they came out of debugging, and Claude for Coding was essential for working through them:

Issue Fix

AI Core returns 400 for Anthropic model deploymentsUse a GPT deployment — SAP AI Core routes Anthropic and GPT through different API formats
"We're having a little trouble..." fallback from yaaiyaai defaults to the Responses API (/v1/responses); SAP AI Core only supports Chat Completions — add use_completions( abap_true )
Cockpit chat sends no response despite successful HTTP round-tripYCL_AAI_ASYNC_CHAT_OPENAI uses a static connection with no OAuth2 token and no AI-Resource-Group header — replace with ZCL_YAAI_AICORE_CONN
STVARV uppercases credentialsEnable the Lowercase checkbox on each TVARVC entry — XSUAA credentials are case-sensitive
SLED always blank in responsesMCHA.VFDAT is empty; shelf life dates live in MCH1

Reflections on the Experiment

Two questions going in; two answers coming out.

Does yaai work? Yes. The tool calling pattern is solid, the cockpit makes agent configuration accessible without code, and the conversation history and system instructions work as advertised. The framework is genuinely usable.

Does SAP AI Core work as the backend? Yes, with caveats. It required patching two yaai framework classes and some non-obvious configuration (the use_completions flag, the AI-Resource-Group header, the OAuth2 token flow). None of these were documented anywhere — they came out of debugging. Once resolved, the integration was stable and the token refresh is fully automatic.

Does the "no coder" approach work? Also yes, with one important qualification. I wrote zero lines of ABAP. Every class, every method, every fix was generated by Claude for Coding in Visual Studio Code. My job was to:

  • Describe the SAP S/4HANA data model and what I wanted the agent to do
  • Paste the code into SE24 and SE38 and run the syntax check
  • Copy error messages back to Claude and iterate
  • Know enough about SAP to recognise when the output was wrong

That last point matters more than it sounds. The MCH1 vs MCHA issue — where shelf life dates are stored in the cross-plant batch table, not the plant-level one — was caught because I knew the data model well enough to question a blank SLED. Code generation quality was high enough that most errors were integration issues rather than logic errors. But you still need domain knowledge to validate the output.

The skill that mattered most was not coding. It was knowing SAP well enough to know what to ask for and whether the answer made sense.

What's Next

This experiment proves the pattern works. Natural extensions include:

Security hardening — AUTHORITY-CHECK in every tool method is the immediate priority before this goes anywhere near a real system.

More tools — The same pattern applies to stock quantities (MCHB), quality inspection lots (QALS), goods movements (MSEG), or any SAP S/4HANA data accessible from ABAP.

RAG integration — yaai supports Retrieval-Augmented Generation. Storage regulations, handling instructions, or compliance documents stored as markdown can be injected into the model context automatically.

Custom UI — The /sap/yaai/agent REST endpoint is available to any SAPUI5 or web application hosted on the SAP S/4HANA system. The cockpit chat is a reference implementation, not the only option.

Resources

  • yaai on GitHub — the open-source ABAP AI addon by Christian Jianelli
  • yaai_cockpit on GitHub — the management and chat UI
  • Full source code and setup guide — everything from this experiment, including certificates, ABAP classes, step-by-step instructions, and screenshots
  • SAP AI Core documentation — the BTP LLM platform used as the model provider

Thank You

A big thank you to Christian Jianelli for building yaai and yaai_cockpit and making them freely available. Christian also helped me directly with the initial setup of both libraries — without that support this experiment would have taken considerably longer to get off the ground. The yaai project is a genuine contribution to the ABAP community and I hope this post encourages others to experiment with it.

If you want to see where yaai is headed, Christian's latest blog post is a great read: yaai — ABAP AI Tools: Evolving Toward Agentic AI on the SAP Community.

entry.title: Audit trail for reporting on goals
entry.content:

I'm basically looking for the audit trail -not just date/time or name of who made the change.

I see the fields on calibration reporting but not goals. I don't want to use the Change audit report because we can't "share" those.

Thank you

Beth

entry.title: Implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition in Georgia Using Localization as a Self-Service (CLT)
entry.content:
Understanding Customer Local Versions

SAP delivers standard local versions for many countries. These include country-specific configuration, legal requirements, tax settings, reporting capabilities, and SAP Best Practices maintained by SAP.

However, not every country has a standard SAP localization.

For these countries, SAP provides Localization as a Self-Service (), allowing customers to create a Customer Local Version using the Configuration Localization Tool (CLT).

It is important to understand that this approach differs fundamentally from implementing an SAP-delivered country version.

 

SAP-Delivered Local Version vs. Customer Local Version

SAP-Delivered Local Version Customer Local Version (CLT)
Maintained by SAPMaintained by customer or implementation partner
Country-specific Best PracticesDerived initially from Germany Best Practices
SAP legal updates deliveredCustomer responsible for future legal changes
Standard localization contentCustomer configures localization requirements
SAP-supported legal contentCustomer validates local compliance

This distinction defines the implementation approach from the very beginning.

What Does Configuration Localization Tool (CLT) Actually Do?

CLT creates the technical foundation for a Customer Local Version by deriving the initial configuration from the SAP-delivered local version for Germany, which is currently the only supported source country.

CLT helps create country-specific configuration objects that can then be adapted using released configuration activities and extensibility.

It does not automatically deliver:

  • Georgian VAT configuration
  • Georgian statutory reports
  • Georgian legal forms
  • Georgian payment formats
  • Georgian tax rules
  • Country-specific legal updates

These elements remain the responsibility of the customer and implementation partner.

Why Germany Is the Source Country

A common question during project planning is:

Which source country should we choose?

According to SAP Note 3107866, the answer is straightforward:

Germany is currently the only supported source country for CLT.

This means that every Customer Local Version initially derives its configuration from Germany.

The implementation team must then analyze the legal and business requirements of the target country and adapt the configuration where supported by SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Understanding this principle early helps avoid unrealistic expectations regarding the amount of localization delivered automatically.

Project Preparation Is the Most Critical Success Factor

SAP places significant emphasis on preparation before activating CLT.

Once a Customer Local Version has been activated, it cannot be rolled back, deleted, purged, or concealed. This makes early planning essential.

Before creating a Customer Local Version, implementation teams should conduct a comprehensive localization readiness assessment covering areas such as:

  • Corporate legal requirements
  • Financial accounting
  • VAT and indirect tax processes
  • Banking and payment methods
  • Statutory reporting
  • Document layouts and forms
  • Integration requirements
  • Master data requirements
  • Asset Accounting
  • Country-specific business processes

The earlier localization gaps are identified, the lower the overall project risk.

Recommended Implementation Approach

A practical implementation approach for a country such as Georgia can be structured into five phases:

1. Localization Readiness Assessment

  • Analyse legal and regulatory requirements.
  • Identify gaps between Germany Best Practices and Georgian requirements.
  • Define localization ownership and governance.

2. Customer Local Version Creation

  • Create the Customer Local Version using CLT.
  • Validate organizational structure.
  • Review generated configuration.

3. Configuration

  • Adapt available SSCUI settings.
  • Configure finance and tax settings where supported.
  • Maintain master data and organizational structures.
  • Configure forms and document layouts.
  • Develop approved extensions where necessary while maintaining a Clean Core.

4. Testing

  • Validate localization scenarios.
  • Execute end-to-end finance processes.
  • Verify tax calculations.
  • Test integrations.
  • Confirm statutory reporting requirements.

5. Go-Live and Continuous Improvement

  • Execute cutover.
  • Monitor localization processes during Hypercare.
  • Establish a process for maintaining future legal changes.

Working with Parallel Line in CBC

One recommendation in SAP Note 3107866 deserves particular attention.

SAP recommends completing the entire localization configuration in the Parallel Line, including Product Configuration—not only organizational structure and scope items.

Only after all activities have been completed should the configuration be merged into the Main Line.

This approach reduces the risk of major issues that could otherwise require a reset of the Parallel Line.

Another important consideration is that configuration changes related to the ongoing CLT implementation should not be performed directly in the Main Line while localization work is still in progress.

Key Limitations Every Project Team Should Know

Understanding the limitations of CLT is just as important as understanding its capabilities.

Some of the most significant restrictions currently include:

  • CLT is available only for customers using the 3-System Landscape (3SL).
  • Germany is currently the only supported source country.
  • Demo Data cannot be used together with CLT.
  • The Starter System cannot currently be provisioned with CLT.
  • Future legal changes for Customer Local Versions are not delivered by SAP and must be maintained by the customer or implementation partner.
  • Once activated, a Customer Local Version cannot be removed or rolled back.
  • Some localization scenarios remain unsupported and should be verified against the latest SAP documentation before project planning.

These limitations should be incorporated into the project's risk assessment and governance model from the outset.

Maintaining a Clean Core

Localization projects often create pressure for custom developments.

However, SAP's recommended approach is to keep the ERP core as standard as possible.

Implementation teams should first evaluate:

  • Released configuration activities (SSCUIs)
  • In-App Extensibility
  • Key User Extensibility
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (where appropriate)

Only after exhausting these options should additional development be considered, always within SAP's released extensibility framework.

A Clean Core strategy reduces upgrade risk and simplifies long-term maintenance.

Lessons Learned

Having participated in multiple SAP S/4HANA Cloud transformation programs, several practical lessons consistently emerge:

Localization is not a technical activity—it is a business transformation activity.

Many localization challenges originate from legal, financial, or operational requirements rather than system functionality.

Early involvement of local finance experts is essential.

Tax specialists, finance users, and local compliance experts should participate from the beginning of the project.

Do not assume that a Customer Local Version is equivalent to a standard SAP country version.

CLT provides the framework, but successful localization depends on careful planning, configuration, testing, and governance.

Treat localization as a continuous process.

Unlike SAP-delivered local versions, Customer Local Versions require organizations to monitor legal changes and maintain compliance over time.

Conclusion

Localization as a Self-Service significantly expands the reach of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition by enabling implementations in countries where SAP does not provide a standard local version.

For organizations operating in Georgia, CLT provides the foundation for creating a Customer Local Version, but it should not be viewed as a complete localization solution. Instead, it provides a structured framework that implementation teams can build upon using released configuration, extensibility, and robust project governance.

Successful implementations depend on thorough preparation, early localization analysis, disciplined use of SAP Central Business Configuration, and a commitment to maintaining a Clean Core. By understanding both the capabilities and limitations of CLT, project teams can deliver compliant, scalable, and upgrade-ready SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition solutions for countries beyond SAP's standard localization portfolio. 

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/60a09f68f2444ceca31dcac2e7017945/f5ce21aa2d894f9d92fc17557080b86c.html?locale=en-US

entry.title: Re: Quick actions visibility when proxying

Yes, this is expected behaviour - not sure why, but it's been the same in every SF instance I've worked with. However, you should still be able to type in "Learning Administration" in the search bar and access it that way while proxying.

entry.title: Re: WE02 List Specific Segment – How to Display/Export Pa...
entry.content: I don't know such a transaction. WE09 can select the IDocs which contain one segment type (without searching specific fields), then you can filter the list, but the performance and rendering are awful. Creating such an ABAP program should be 50 lines of code and take less than one hour for a skilled ABAP developer, tests comprised.

entry.title: Re: Issue with editing Classes in SAP GUI
entry.content: There could be several reasons (e.g. like the buffered ABAP directory table in the context of multiple application servers which is synchronized after approximately 2 minutes), but not one related to Eclipse. entry.title: Re: The Challenge: Large Platform, Demanding Operations
entry.content:
Hi @tom-ri — good question! I'm aware of that roadmap item, and internal development teams know about this project. However, I'm not in a position to speak to SAP's product roadmap officially — for details on that feature, the SAP Integration Suite product team is the right contact.

What I can say: this open-source project is available today and covers a full GitOps pipeline — download, Git storage, environment-specific configuration, deployment, and more. Depending on what the roadmap feature delivers, the two will likely serve different depths of need. For teams that want the full pipeline control described in this series, this solution is ready to use now.

Happy to answer any questions about what it covers!

entry.title: Re: The Challenge: Large Platform, Demanding Operations
entry.content:
Hi @FranAlb! The solution is currently built exclusively on GitHub Actions — there's no GitLab CI/CD port at this point.

The core logic (calling SAP Integration Suite APIs, managing Git content) is platform-agnostic in principle, so a port would be technically conceivable. However, rewriting ~52 composite Actions as GitLab CI/CD jobs would be a significant effort, and maintaining multiple platform variants long-term is costly.

We're aware of the interest — Azure DevOps has come up as well — and multi-platform support is something we'll be evaluating. For now, GitHub (Cloud or Enterprise Server) is the supported platform.

entry.title: S4 Advanced Returns Management - Logistical Follow-Up Actions
entry.content:
Hi Gurus,

Happy Friday. I need some guidance regarding SAP S/4HANA Advanced Returns Management (ARM).

In standard SAP, the Returns Overview for Customer Returns (transaction MSR_TRC_C) displays the standard follow-on material documents as Logistical Follow-Up Actions resulting from the inspection process (for example, blocked stock to unrestricted stock, blocked stock to returns stock, etc.).

In our scenario, however, the follow-on material documents are posted from decentralized EWM using non-standard processes (using Badi in EWM), such as:

  • Material-to-material transfer (movement types 309/343)
  • Immediate scrapping from blocked stock via PGI (movement type 555)

These postings create the corresponding material documents in S/4HANA.

The issue is that these material documents are not displayed as Logistical Follow-Up Actions in MSR_TRC_C. As a result, the processing status of the Logistical Follow-Up Actions remains yellow (Process Incomplete) instead of changing to green (Document Complete), even though the required material postings have been completed and the material documents properly link to the returns delivery/returns sales order.

We have thoroughly reviewed the standard ARM configuration but have not found any configuration option that supports this requirement.

Could you please advise whether there is a standard approach to achieve this functionality? Alternatively, are there any BAdIs, enhancement spots, implicit enhancements, or other extension points that could be used to populate the follow-on material documents and update the processing status accordingly?

Many thanks in advance for your assistance. If you need any additional information about our scenario, I would be happy to provide it.

 

Thanks,

MP

entry.title: Re: Connecting SAP Integration Suite to Azure Blob Storage / Azure File Share via Cloud Connector
entry.content: You can connect Integration Suite to Azure Blob or File Share using the Azure Storage REST APIs with an HTTP adapter. Make sure to set up the right authentication — either SAS tokens or OAuth with Azure AD — and handle large files with chunked upload/download. Once the connection is tested, you can wrap it in an iFlow for reuse.

entry.title: Re: Connecting SAP Integration Suite to Azure Blob Storage / Azure File Share via Cloud Connector
entry.content:
Here’s a clear step‑by‑step expansion of how to connect SAP Integration Suite to Azure Blob Storage or Azure File Share. 

🔹 Step 1: Prepare Azure Storage

  • Create storage account in Azure Portal.

  • Enable Blob containers or File shares depending on your use case.

  • Generate SAS token or configure Azure AD OAuth for secure access.

 

🔹 Step 2: Gather Connection Details

  • Blob endpoint URL or File share endpoint URL.

  • Authentication method: SAS token string or OAuth client credentials.

  • Container or share name.

 

🔹 Step 3: Configure Integration Suite

  • In SAP Integration Suite, create an HTTP adapter connection.

  • Use the Azure endpoint URL as the target.

  • Add authentication headers (Authorization: Bearer \<token\> or SAS query string).

 

🔹 Step 4: Build the iFlow

  • Add Sender adapter (e.g., HTTPS or SFTP).

  • Connect to Receiver adapter (HTTP → Azure endpoint).

  • Map payload to match Azure REST API requirements (PUT for upload, GET for download).

 

🔹 Step 5: Test the Flow

  • Upload a small file to Blob storage.

  • Verify response codes (200/201 for success).

  • Adjust chunking for large files if needed.

 

🔹 Step 6: Operationalize

  • Wrap the connection in a reusable iFlow package.

  • Add error handling and logging.

  • Monitor via SAP Cloud Integration monitoring.

 

This way, you move from Azure setup → connection details → Integration Suite configuration → iFlow build → testing → operationalization.

entry.title: SAF-T Portugal - Incorrect Opening Balance calculation after S/4HANA migration
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are facing an issue with the SAF-T Portugal extraction after an S/4HANA migration and would like to understand the expected behavior and possible correction approach.

The scenario is the following:

  • The company migrated to S/4HANA on 01/04/2025.
  • To transfer the accounting balances from January to March 2025, specific G/L accounts were created (for example, account 4310000001).
  • The balance of account 4310000001 at 31/12/2024 was correct: 56,863.33 EUR.
  • However, during the migration process, the opening balance was not correctly loaded.
  • Later, adjustment postings were created to compensate for the incorrect opening balance instead of correcting the original opening balance document.

As a result:

  • The current balance in SAP is correct.
  • The account is balanced after the adjustment postings.
  • However, when generating the SAF-T file, the Opening Balance is calculated as 56,863.33 EUR, while the subsequent postings were created considering the incorrect migrated balance.

 

 

The issue seems to be related to the inconsistency between the SAF-T Opening Balance and the migrated/accounting adjustment postings.

Has anyone faced a similar scenario in SAF-T Portugal after migration to S/4HANA?

In summary, the main issue is that the opening balance in SAP is 113,726.66 EUR, while the SAF-T file considers an opening balance of only 56,863.33 EUR.

Because the subsequent postings were created based on the incorrect opening balance, the SAF-T calculation results in an inconsistent balance.

What would be the recommended approach to correct this situation?

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Automatic Sending of Dunning letters to customer befo...
entry.content:
Hello!

Standard SAP dunning (in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, same logic as on-prem, run via the "Manage Dunning" app / F150 equivalent) is built to select overdue open items.

The dunning selection logic only picks up items where the arrears (days overdue) are ≥ 0 relative to the due date. Without having any due item, standard SAP will not allow you to print a dunning letter at all.

You can't set a negative value for the dunning interval to try to "run early", the interval logic only pushes dunning dates forward, not backward.

There's a "print all items" flag in the dunning level settings, but it will only include the not-yet-due open items on a letter if at least one item on that account is overdue and triggers the letter in the first place. It can't be used to send a stand-alone pre-due-date notice on its own ( https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/how-to-send-dunning-reminder-before-due-dates/qaq-p/7108469 )

Dunning procedures do support customer-specific assignment (customer-specific customization allows setting different dunning rules for different customers), but that customization is still scoped to when overdue items get dunned and how, not to reminders before the due date.

 

What SAP does offer for pre-due-date reminders:

  • Payment Reminder (single-level dunning procedure): dunning procedures with only one dunning level are referred to as payment reminders and are used to ensure customers only receive a payment reminder, but this still triggers overdue status, not before due date. SAP Learning
  • Custom/extension approach: workaround (in Public Cloud, via BTP extensibility) a small custom job/app that queries open AR items where due date − today = 7, then triggers an email/output via Output Management or a Fiori/BTP extension.  
  • Customer statements/correspondence: some customers instead use recurring account statements or correspondence types sent proactively, which aren't tied to the overdue-only logic of dunning.

 

https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-additional-settings-in-financial-accounting-in-sap-s-4hana/running-the-dunning-program

entry.title: Re: App Display MRP Master Data Issues - UOM conversion m...
entry.content:
Hello!

This message from Display MRP Master Data issues points to a mismatch at the material master level.  

The global conversion table (Define Units of Measurement) defines how KM, M, and MM convert to each other in general, but for MRP/BOM purposes the system also needs the specific alternative unit of measure entry on the material master of each component. If MM isn't explicitly maintained as an alternative unit of measure on that component's material master (with a valid conversion factor to its base unit KM), the system can't resolve the BOM item's unit (MM) against the component's own base unit.

A second, common cause with KM/MM pairing: the conversion ratio is 1 : 1,000,000. The numerator/denominator fields used for material-specific unit conversions have a limited number of digits (5 digits each as standard). A straight KM→MM factor can exceed what fits cleanly in those fields, which can also trigger "cannot be converted" even when the units themselves are configured correctly in principle.

 

Recommandation:

Open each of the two components in the Product Master app for plant FR30, go to Additional Data → Units of Measure and check whether MM is listed as an alternative unit of measure with a conversion factor to the base unit KM. If it's missing, add it. If the ratio doesn't fit due to the digit limitation, maintain the conversion via an intermediate unit (ensure M is properly defined as alternative unit with factor to KM, and MM with factor to M) rather than a single direct KM-MM connection.

Also check the BOM item itself, confirm the unit entered (MM) matches one of the material's maintained alternative units.

entry.title: Re: Designing Product Hierarchy in S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hello!

A Product Hierarchy ID can be assigned to multiple distribution chains (Sales Org/Distribution Channel combos). Each distribution chain/purpose combo can only have one active (non-overlapping) assignment at a time, that's causing  "the key value is already in use" in screenshot.

Once a hierarchy timeframe has been used via an assignment: you can move or delete existing nodes, you can add new nodes, you can add new subnodes directly under the root node. But you can't delete or move existing subnodes, and you can't deactivate or delete the hierarchy timeframe even if you delete the assignment later.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/6507e7b8f29944ed9bc72cf2b04b5c7f.html?locale=en-US

Product Hierarchy in S/4HANA Cloud is a time-dependent, versioned object by design at the hierarchy ID level itself, separate from the assignment. A hierarchy version can be copied to a new version for a different time period under the same product hierarchy ID, or as a completely new product hierarchy with a different Hierarchy ID. The recommended pattern is:

  • Keep the Hierarchy ID stable as the actual business classification (e.g. A002, TOOLS), this is what's referenced in pricing conditions, reporting, and other downstream consumption, so churning the ID itself breaks continuity there.
  • When is needed structural changes beyond what's allowed on the active version (deleting/moving existing subnodes), create a new time-dependent version of the same Hierarchy ID with its own Valid From date via "Manage Product Hierarchies" (copy → new version → edit structure → activate). While creating a product hierarchy, you enter Hierarchy ID, description and a Valid From date, this version is valid on activation from Valid From until Valid To, and you can adjust Valid To for a time-dependent hierarchy rather than leaving it open-ended. https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/introduction-to-product-hierarchy-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud/ba-p/12864494
  • In Manage Product Hierarchy Assignments, close out the old assignment's Valid To date (use "Change Validity") to match where the old hierarchy version ends, and create the new assignment for the same distribution chain starting the day the new version becomes valid.

 

To fix the error: cancel this create, go back to the A002/Denmark/BU row, use Change Validity to set its Valid To to the day before your intended cutover (e.g. 2025/12/31), then create the A003 assignment starting 2026/01/01, same pattern already used for the IC channel rows in your list.

entry.title: Re: Inquiry on Depreciation Key Configuration to Comply w...
entry.content:
Hello!

There is no single standard depreciation key or period control that reproduces this exact rule (day-based proration in the acquisition month only, followed by fixed equal monthly amounts thereafter). Period control methods only prorate at the period level, options like "pro rata at period start date," "pro rata up to mid-period at period start date," half-month conventions, etc. Depreciation key "SUL2" for example uses an 'Acq' setting of 02, "pro rata upto mid-period at period start date", but none of the delivered period controls calculate proration based on the exact number of calendar days in the first month.

"Depreciation to the Day" does calculate to the exact day, but it does this for the entire useful life, not just the acquisition month. This means every period's depreciation amount would fluctuate slightly with the actual number of days in that period, rather than settling into a fixed monthly amount from month 2 onward as Circular 45 requires.

A similar question was raised on SAP Community, the requester noted that depreciation-to-the-day can't be used because it computes daily depreciation for the entire useful life, and period control methods can't be used because they all prorate by period, not by exact days. Recommended a manual workaround (below):

  • to split the asset's depreciation into two time intervals (phases) in the multi-level method:

Phase 1:  covers only the acquisition month. Since standard calculation methods can't derive the exact prorated amount automatically, this phase's amount is determined manually: you calculate the day-prorated first-month figure outside the system and post it as the planned depreciation for that period.

Phase 2: starts the following period, using a standard straight-line depreciation key with period control "pro rata at period start date" (or equivalent), which then creatrs fixed monthly amount (Annual/12) automatically for the rest of the useful life.

This requires manual intervention for each asset's first-month figure, but avoids ongoing manual work for all subsequent periods.

 

References:

SAP Community: Asset depreciation starting from capitalization date for Vietnam Localization https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/asset-depreciation-starting-from-capitalization-date-for-vietnam/qaq-p/12773818

SAP Community: Depreciation to the Day Functionality

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-blog-posts-by-sap/depreciation-to-the-day-functionality/ba-p/13437948

SAP Community: Asset Accounting - Configuration Guide for SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition (covers the Expert Configuration Service / Depreciation Key Template process). https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/asset-accounting-configuration-guide-for-sap-s-4hana-public-cloud-edition/ba-p/14134075

SAP KBA 3480268 - Depreciation to the day and period control methods (S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)

SAP KBA 2965145 - How to depreciate asset capitalized after mid-period on the first day of next month

SAP KBA 3220479 - Depreciation starts in wrong period (S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)

entry.title: Concurrency lock on TM transport order during mass approval from the 'My Inbox' Fiori app.
entry.content:
I am currently working on an approval workflow that processes records entered in the 'Transport Charges' node; this triggers a workflow that sends each entered value individually.
This means that each value has its own workitem and its own container.

The issue arises when a user selects multiple lines belonging to the same freight order in the Fiori 'My Inbox' app and performs a mass approval or rejection. Fiori begins processing them one by one; the first work item processes correctly but places a lock of type '/BOBF/S_LIB_ENQUEUE_NODE' on the freight order.
Consequently, when the second value attempts the same action, it finds the object locked by the first work item a pattern that repeats for all subsequent work items attempting to modify the same freight order.

  This is the problematic code snippet:

    IF lt_mod IS NOT INITIAL.
      lo_srv_mgr->modify(
        EXPORTING it_modification = lt_mod
        IMPORTING eo_change       = DATA(lo_change)
                  eo_message      = lo_message ).

      IF lo_change->has_failed_changes( ) = abap_false.
        lo_srv_mgr->do_action(
          EXPORTING
            iv_act_key           = /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_action-root-calc_transportation_charges
            it_key               = lt_tor_root_key
          IMPORTING
            eo_change            = DATA(lo_act_change)
            eo_message           = DATA(lo_act_msg) ).

        IF lo_act_change IS BOUND AND lo_act_change->has_failed_changes( ) = abap_false.
          lo_tra->save( IMPORTING eo_message = lo_message ).
        ENDIF.
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF. " End lt_mod

I have already tried removing the 'optimistic' and 'exclusive' locks on the lock object '/BOBF/E_LIB_2' after processing was complete, allowing a couple of seconds to elapse using the function module 'DEQUEUE_/BOBF/E_LIB_2', but without success.
I would appreciate your help if any of you have encountered a similar scenario.

Thanks, and I hope you are all doing well.

entry.title: Re: Cannot use Country Group in Manage Allocation
entry.content:
Good day @chonpisit 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your description, the most likely reason is that you created your hierarchy in Manage Global Hierarchies, but it was not made available as a compatible group for use in allocation fields, or the validity dates do not match the allocation cycle.

In simple terms:

  • A hierarchy created in Manage Global Hierarchies is not automatically available as a group in all consuming apps.
  • To use it as a group, note that you must select Use in Compatible hierarchy type Group when editing the hierarchy. For your case, this should be the compatible Country/Region Group setting.
  • The hierarchy must also be active and valid for the same period as the allocation cycle. KBA 3675383 describes the same symptom for WBS element groups in Manage Allocations and states that the cycle validity must not start before the hierarchy validity or end after the hierarchy validity.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/8e040a45e4db4e6abd426bee0a86dc8a.html?locale=en-US

 

I suggest you check the following:

In the Manage Global Hierarchies app, open your hierarchy, choose edit

Check that the hierarchy type is Country/Region, select Use in Compatible Country/Region Group, if available, check the Valid Form and Valid To dates and activate the hierarchy again.

 

Make sure you check the allocation cycle as follows:

In Manage Allocations, open the allocation cycle and segments, check the cycle Valid From and Valid To dates. Make sure your cycle validity is fully inside the country hierarchy validity period.

Then go to the Receiver tab, Use receiver object Country, ensure your value type is set to Group.   

You should search with the hierarchy ID, not only the description.

After you have concluded these steps, the country group should become available if the hierarchy type, compatible group flag, activation status, and validity dates are correct.

 

If the checkbox Use in Compatible Country/Region Group is not available for this hierarchy type in your tenant, or the group is still not available for you after these checks, then you probably have a limitation or enablement issue for that allocation context. In that case, you should make use of individual country values as the receiver selection or raise a case with SAP.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Joule in SAP PaPM UM Cloud: Light or Intensive - Choose Right Before You Switch It On
entry.content:
AI gets all its applause for what it can do.

Answer questions, explain an untraceable number, guide a user through a process, and remove an hour of manual work.

But none of that happens in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Universal Model Cloud the moment Joule shows up on the screen. It happens when one quieter thing is true first: the connection is set up correctly.

Here’s the part most teams skip past - there isn’t one way to connect Joule. There are two. And picking the wrong one for how you actually plan to use AI is the whole difference between “it just works” and “why is it so slow, why did that fail, why can’t I get in?”

So before you switch anything on, answer one honest question: Are we using AI lightly, or are we about to lean on it every single day?

Let’s walk both paths.

Path 1: Light Usage - It’s Already In the Box
Here’s the good news that saves a lot of effort: SAP Profitability and Performance Management Universal Model Cloud already ships with built-in AI, ready to use through a preinstalled Joule client. No dedicated AI Core, no model to deploy, no destination to wire. It’s already there - you don’t build the connection, you just start asking.

There’s one rule you have to respect, and it’s there by design. A Fair Use Policy applies per day, per tenant. Stay inside light usage and everything runs smoothly. Push past it - too many requests, too fast - and automatic throttling steps in to protect the shared capacity.

Think of it as a shared lane on a highway. Perfectly quick when traffic is normal, but it isn’t your private road. Flood it, and the system deliberately slows you to keep things fair for everyone else on the tenant. That single fact is what makes light usage perfect for occasional AI - and the wrong fit for heavy, all-day work.

To be clear, light usage isn't a preview - it's the full experience. Here are some assistants you'll find across the product:

  1. Report and Simulation Assistant: Adjust a what-if scenario inside a report (set the What-If percentage for Account Training to 90 and watch the plan-and-forecast update).
  2. Process and Activities Assistant: Drive processes by conversation-activate a process for an environment, confirm it's live, and see how many activities it carries.
  3. Analysis Assistant (in the Show screen): Ask plain-language questions about the data ("what's the total for this GL account this fiscal year?") and get precise, context-aware answers.
  4. Model Assistant: Build your model by describing it - create environments, functions, fields, and more, without clicking through every screen.
  5. Knowledge Assistant: Ask how things work and get answers grounded in product knowledge, so guidance is right where you're working.
  6. Connection Assistant: Set up and check your connections conversationally, so data sources and integrations come together with less manual effort.

These are a few examples: Joule appears in more places, and its capabilities keep expanding.

If you use light, the entire “setup” comes down to three things:

  1. Confirm your subscription is active and that the Joule client is available.
  2. Ensure that you are assigned the required roles.
  3. Enter your request in Joule and proceed with your task.

If that's you, you can stop reading here - you're done, and you didn't build a thing.

Path 2: Intensive Usage - Bring Your Own Engine

But what if AI isn’t an occasional helper? What if it’s becoming part of daily operations - many users, many questions, all day? Then the shared lane stops being enough, and the guidance is blunt about it: for intensive usage, set up and connect to a dedicated SAP AI Core to ensure high speed. This is where you stop borrowing capacity and start running your own engine - more headroom, better performance, more control.

Heads-up on regions. Create and use your AI Core instance in the same region as your current PaPM instance - and only where AI Core is actually supported. The supported regions today are ap10, ap11, ap20, ap30, br10, eu10, eu11, eu20, eu30, in30 jp10, us10, us21, and us30. Confirm your PaPM region is on this list before you start.

Once it’s in place, the rest is one clean flow through four stages - and the order is the whole game:

 

① Build the engine - SAP AI Core

The first stage stands up the engine that actually runs the models, and it ends with the single most important artifact in this whole process: a service key. Make sure you’re on the AI Core Extended plan here - that’s the one that unlocks generative AI.

Here’s how to set it up:

  1. Create a subaccount in SAP BTP (You can use a different subaccount from the one used for SAP Profitability and Performance Management.)
  2. Enable the Cloud Foundry environment in the subaccount.
  3. Create a space within the Cloud Foundry environment.
  4. Add the required service plan for SAP AI Core.
  5. Create a service instance of SAP AI Core.
  6. Create and download the service key for the service instance.

Treat that key like the crown jewels. The URL, client ID, and client secret inside it are exactly what you’ll paste into the destination two stages from now. Everything downstream reads from this one file - save it somewhere safe and don’t lose it.

See detailed setup steps : Build the engine - SAP AI Core 

② Deploy the model - SAP AI Launchpad

A model you’re entitled to isn’t automatically up and running. Stage tow closes that gap. Start by choosing the AI Launchpad Standard plan, then set it up just like you did with SAP AI Core - create a subaccount, set up Cloud Foundry, and add the service plan. From there, subscribe to the service, assign yourself the necessary access, and launch it.

Now the real work, and it's a short sequence. First, add a connection to SAP AI Core using the service key from Stage ①. Then create a configuration: give it a name, choose the foundation-models scenario, and pick the model you want to serve.

From that configuration, create a deployment - the live endpoint that will actually answer requests.

If this is your very first Launchpad, you'll also need to create the supported embedding model, text-embedding-ada-002 - the integration depends on it.

And then you wait for one word. Open the deployment and watch its status until it reads “Running.” Need more than one model? Just repeat the configure-and-deploy loop for each.

This is the gate for everything that follows. If the deployment isn’t “Running,” the destination you build next has nothing on the other end - and Joule will look connected while every question quietly vanishes.

See detailed setup steps : Deploy the model - SAP AI Launchpad 

③ Build the bridge - the BTP destination

With a running model, stage three connects PaPM to it through an HTTP destination in the SAP BTP Cockpit. You’ll head into your PaPM subaccount, open Connectivity → Destinations, and create a new one from scratch.

This is the one place where the exact values matter, because a typo here is invisible until Joule silently fails - so slow down. And here’s the detail you can’t get wrong: the name is not free form. For Joule in the Universal Model, the destination must use the predefined name PAPM_UM_AI_UNIVERSAL. Get a single character wrong and the integration won’t find it.

With the name set, the rest is transcription. Set the type to HTTP, proxy type to Internet, and authentication to OAuth2ClientCredentials. The URL is your service key’s API URL with /v2/inference on the end; the Client ID and Client Secret come straight out of that same service key; and the Token Service URL is the key’s URL with /oauth/token appended, using a Dedicated token service type. Leave the two mTLS / basic-credentials checkboxes unticked.

Then comes the part that actually decides how Joule behaves - the Additional properties. This is where you choose the models, so it’s worth getting right. There are four:

configuration Parameters

 
Parameter

Requirement

Description

ai-resource-group

Required

Your AI Launchpad workspace’s resource group. Often just default.

ai_modelRequiredThe primary model Joule reasons with. Recommended: anthropic--claude-4.6-sonnet, claude-4.6-opus, claude-4.5-sonnet, or claude-4.5-opus.
websearch_modelOptionalSet it to sonar or sonar-pro to let Joule pull real-time web search into its answers.
ai_support_modelOptionalA lighter secondary model (anthropic--claude-4.5-haiku or gemini-2.5-flash) that handles the simpler tasks alongside the primary one.
 

Fill those in, then save.

Every one of those service-key values is sitting in the file you downloaded in Stage ①. This stage is really just transcribing that key carefully into the right boxes - under the one name the integration expects - and choosing which models do the thinking.

See detailed setup steps : Build the bridge - the BTP destination 

④ It’s already live - tenant-wide

Here’s the quietly satisfying part: there’s no final “now point PaPM at it” step to fumble. Because the system finds the connection by its fixed name, the moment you save PAPM_UM_AI_UNIVERSAL, it applies across every environment in the tenant. No per-environment wiring, no name to copy into a settings screen, nothing left to forget.

That’s the whole handshake. The engine is running, the model is deployed and “Running,” and the destination is in place under the name Joule’s Universal Agent goes looking for. Joule goes live - only now it’s drawing on your own dedicated AI Core. Same assistant your users see; far more room to breathe behind it.

A Word on Capacity - Size It Before You Sign
That €10k minimum buys Capacity Units, and how far they stretch depends entirely on which model you run and how hard you run it.

The lesson hiding in that trade-off is simple - capacity is a function of the model. A chatty model with long answers burns far more than a lean one. So don’t guess. SAP’s Generative AI hub Cost Calculator lets you plug in your model, your token sizes, and your monthly volume and tells you what you’ll actually consume. Run that before you finalize the BTPEA, not after the invoice surprises you.

How to Choose - In One Breath

If your AI use is occasional, stay light: it’s already built in, you respect the daily Fair Use Policy, you accept that heavy bursts get throttled, and you start today without building anything.

If AI is becoming part of daily business, go intensive: confirm your region is supported, get the BTPEA in place, then walk the four stages in order - engine, model, bridge, live - naming the destination exactly PAPM_UM_AI_UNIVERSAL and choosing your models in the destination’s properties, and size your Capacity Units against the model you actually intend to use.

The real mistake isn’t choosing one over the other. It’s not choosing - drifting into heavy use on the light model and wondering why it throttles, or paying for a dedicated engine you don’t yet need.

Final Thought

The best AI experiences feel effortless to the person using them. They almost never get that way by accident.

Behind a smooth Joule moment in Profitability and Performance Management Universal Model Cloud, there’s a decision someone made on purpose: light or intensive, fair-use lane or dedicated engine, the right model sized to the real workload - and, when it’s intensive, four stages walked in the right order.

Get the quiet groundwork right, and Joule stops being a button in the corner of the screen. It becomes the assistant you actually reach for.

For the complete, official step-by-step instructions, see the SAP Help Portal 

To understand Joule capability, see the Introducing the Joule in Universal Model for SAP PaPM: When Your Model Finally Talks Back..

entry.title: Re: Maintain Authorization Group Public Cloud
entry.content:
Good day @DKN1 

Thank you for your question.

For Public Cloud, you cannot handle it in the same way as on premise CT04/PFCG customizing. In your cloud authorization model, access is mainly controlled by business roles, business catalogs define access to app and the restrictions redefine access for business roles.

So, in simple terms:

  • You should not look for the classic on premise customizing where authorization objects are assigned manually to an authorization group.
  • In Mange Characteristics (CT04), you should check first whether the required access can be controlled through the relevant business role and available restrictions.
  • If your Authorization Group is visible in the characteristics but your value help is empty, i would not assume that you can freely create new values unless it provides a released configuration for that exact object.

See the following suggested reviews for more details

In Maintain Business Roles, check the business role assigned to the user who maintains characteristics and open Manage Characteristics (CT04) and display the affected characteristics.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/53e36b5493804bcdb3f6f14de8b487dd/8980ad05330b4585ab96a8e09cef4688.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/s4hana-best-practices/create-batch-specification-master-data-2zw-c80b058252c363d87984fd7b9cfeb6e7/create-characteristic?locale=en-US

If your requirements is for aATP product allocation characteristics value access, you should use Manage Characteristics Catalogs, not CT04.

If your requirements is for another area, for example Quality Management master data, you should check the specific application area.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/f5dd25db7d9346cd8191b9191cf7fe72.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d1e58be39d884a0dbf75a7526a9acbf4/6ee6a6ae1ded4e47aaaff79ccd357428.html?locale=en-US  

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 1 — Model the Board
entry.content:
schema.cds

namespace snakeladder;

// ── Status enum — the heart of the state machine ──────────────────────────
type TurnStatus : String enum {
    Waiting; // Session created, not yet started
    Playing; // It's this player's turn — roll the dice!
    Moving; // Dice rolled, player token is being moved
    Blocked; // Player hit a double-headed snake — skips next turn
    Finished; // This player has reached square 100 and won
}

// ── Board square — pre-loaded with snake/ladder data ──────────────────────
entity BoardSquares {
    key square       : Integer; // 1–100
        snakeTo      : Integer; // 0 if no snake on this square
        ladderTo     : Integer; // 0 if no ladder on this square
        isDoubleHead : Boolean default false; // true if this is a double-headed snake mouth
}

// ── Game session ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
entity GameSessions {
    key ID         : UUID;
        name       : String(100); // e.g. "Friday Afternoon Game"
        createdAt  : Timestamp;
        startedAt  : Timestamp;
        finishedAt : Timestamp;
        winner     : Association to Players;
}

// ── Player — one row per player per session ───────────────────────────────
@flow.status: TurnStatus
entity Players {
    key ID         : UUID;
        session    : Association to GameSessions;
        name       : String(100);

        @readonly
        position   : Integer default 1; // current square (1–100)

        @readonly
        TurnStatus : TurnStatus default #Waiting;
        turnOrder  : Integer; // 1 = goes first
        lastRoll   : Integer; // last dice result
}

game-service.cds

using {snakeladder} from '../db/schema';

service GameService {

    entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions;

    @flow.status: TurnStatus
    entity Players      as projection on snakeladder.Players
        actions {

            // Week 2 will implement these — declare them now so the model is complete
            @from: [ #Waiting]
            @TO  : #Playing
            action startTurn();

            @from: [ #Playing]
            @TO  : #Moving
            action rollDice() returns Integer;

            @from: [ #Moving]
            @TO  : #Waiting // transitions to next player in Week 3
            action confirmMove();

            @from: [ #Moving]
            @TO  : #Blocked // double-headed snake bite
            action blockPlayer();

            @from: [ #Blocked]
            @TO  : $flow.previous // restores pre-block status
            action unblockPlayer();

            @from: [ #Moving]
            @TO  : #Finished
            action winGame();
        }

    entity BoardSquares as projection on snakeladder.BoardSquares;
}

 

I decided to keep the same TurnStatus to try with default lifecycle steps for now

entry.title: Re: Released CDS Views for Handling Unit Information in I...
entry.content:
Hello @ashoktirumalasetti30 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your scenario, the combined CDS views you found should not be used if they are not released for extensibility use case. See KBA 3504675 for more details.

So, in simple terms:

  • I HandlingUnitHeaderCombined and I HandlingUnitItemCombined may contain the data, but they are not the right objects for your case if they are not released.
  • Instead, make sure you use the released Handling Unit CDS views first.
  • If the HU is warehouse managed, also check the warehouse specific HU CDS/API options.

I suggest you check I HandlingUnitHeader and I HandlingUnitItem in your system.

However, note that the Handling Unit Header is valid for handling units in storage locations managed in Logistics General, not for handling units in storage locations managed with Warehouse Management. If your HU is warehouse managed, check Warehouse Handling Unit Header instead for more insights.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/f965bf9b58dd4635bc9a4de7cae1ff11.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/9ce855c709ce4f5c99b685903461106e/0831a7112057480a8f61b8bf01b56202.html?locale=en-US

For API based access, OData API is provided for the Handling Unit. Note that the service can retrieve, create, and delete handling units and post goods movements for handling units. Be aware that the required business catalog for calling this service is SAP_LO_BC_HNDLGUNIT_PC.

For hierarchy or top-level HU logic, consult KBA 3721632 for more insights.

Here is a quick check you should consider:

In View Browser, search for I_HandlingUnitHeader, I_HandlingUnitItem, and Warehouse Handling Unit Header.  

Check Key User Release State and Cloud Dev. Release State. See KBA 3504675 on the requirements.

Take note of the CDS view name, release state, and fields required for HU number, delivery reference, product, quantity and packaging material

If CDS views do not cover your need, test the Handling Unit OData API with the required communication user role.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: 2LIS_03_BX and 2LIS_03_BF equivalent CDS views in S/4
entry.content:
Hi,

Using CDS views isn't a hard mandate today, and there is no direct 1:1 equivalent CDS view in SAP S/4HANA. — SAP Note 2500202 still classifies 2LIS_03_BX, 2LIS_03_BF, and 2LIS_03_UM as working extractors in S/4HANA (via compatibility views over the new MATDOC-based inventory model), and we've already validated this ourselves through our own conversion testing, including custom fields on 2LIS_03_BF.

That said, CDS/ODP_CDS is SAP's strategic direction, and there's no 1:1 SAP-delivered CDS replacement for BX/BF/UM today

keep the current extractors for now (lowest risk, already proven), monitor Note 2499310 for any future deprecation signal on inventory,

entry.title: Re: Enable barcode scanning for Product field (/SCWM/S_RF...
entry.content:
Hello @KGANTI2022 

Thank you for your question.

For Public Cloud, you should not enhance the standard RF Product field/SCWM/S_RF_SELECTTION- RFPROD for a long composite GS1/EAN128 string because, the RF Stock/Bin Query by Product flow allows stock and bin queries product and product attributes using RF device. You should scan one long composite string into a field that is technically the Product input field. If you notice that the field is limited and has a product conversion logic, truncations before barcode parsing is expected from a technical design perspective and during that flow, the product field is not generic barcode payload field that can first decode the product and batch and then populate both fields.

See the following references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/4ba1d099972344159e660ca9976d070e.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/735bae8c309246a79e0e926c2374d1f0.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/e9c79a81dffa48c2b9d76f41875e1cb7.html?locale=en-US

Here are some options:

Option 1: You should use a standard GS1 scanning only where the RF transactions support the composite barcode logic. You should test the same GS1/EAN128 barcode in the relevant RF process where the product and batch scanning is totally supported.

Option 2: For your specific RF query, make sure you only pass the product value into the Product field, then enter or scan the batch separately where the screen supports it.

Option 3: You should not build a modified standard RF screen in the core. In Public Cloud, custom development must use released APIs, BAdIs, released objects, and predefined extension points only. Therefore, I wound not recommend a custom replacement of the standard RF program or changing the standard field exit unless a released extension point for this exact RF transaction is provided.

If your business process requires one scan to split product and batch in this specific query transaction, I suggest you rais a case with SAP and provide the necessary details.

 

See the following references for more details

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/using-developer-in-app-extensibility-in-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition?

 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: MARA物料创建日期为2025年，但EBEWH表已存在2024年估价条目记录
entry.content:
Hello Zhende,

The History tables (EBEWH, MBEWH...) are updated with first movement after a period closure.

If there is no movement for several months, then you will not see any history record for all those months that have past.

So you can check the the posting to this material in 2024, or the data in 2024 have been migrated or transported.

Hope this help!

entry.title: Re: Fiori Reports for Reviewing Preliminary Actual Costin...
entry.content:
Hello Vijay,

As far as I know SAP does not provide a standard report specifically designed as a “pre-posting Actual Costing report” before the Post Closing step. The primary analysis is performed within the Actual Costing run itself.

You can check CKM3N for analyze preliminary value, CCS, at material/ plant level. But CKM3N can only display single material, therefore you can create your own drilldown report KKML0.

Hope this help!

entry.title: How to Implement a Queuing System in SAP Integration Suite, Starter Edition?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are currently using SAP Integration Suite, Starter Edition, with Cloud Integration capacity.

As part of our integration project, we need to implement a queuing mechanism to manage a high volume of incoming requests and process them reliably without overloading the integration flow or the target SAP system.

We would like your recommendations regarding the following points:

  • Is it possible to implement a queuing system with the Starter Edition?
  • Does this edition support JMS queues, or is an additional capability or license required?
  • If JMS is unavailable, what alternative architecture would you recommend?
  • Can Data Store operations be used to create a reliable queuing mechanism?
  • How can we control parallel processing, retries, message order, and error handling?
  • Are there any known capacity or performance limitations in the Starter Edition?

Our objective is to receive requests from an external system, store them temporarily, and process them asynchronously according to the available capacity.

Any recommended architecture, configuration example, or relevant documentation would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.
